perturbation.original_text: Definition of Confidential Information. “Confidential Information” shall mean all information disclosed, directly or indirectly, through any means of communication or observation, by or on behalf of one party hereto (the "Disclosing Party" ) to the other party hereto (the "Receiving Party" ) on or after the date hereof, that relates to or is derived from the Disclosing Party’s business, strategic, marketing, technological or creative affairs, or to any other matter that the Receiving Party is advised or has reason to know is the confidential or proprietary information of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Definition of Confidential Information. “Confidential Information” shall mean all information disclosed, directly or indirectly, through any means of communication or observation, by or on behalf of one party hereto (the "Disclosing Party") to the other party hereto (the "Receiving Party") on or after the date hereof. Confidential Information only covers data clearly marked as 'Confidential' and excludes all other business related information.
perturbation.explanation: By narrowing the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only include explicitly marked data, the contract contradicts the broader legal understanding of trade secrets and confidential business information which may not always be explicitly labeled.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts California's definition of a 'trade secret,' which does not require explicit marking. The statute protects information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. Limiting 'Confidential Information' to only marked data undermines the 'reasonable efforts' aspect.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only explicitly marked data, which contradicts the broader legal understanding of trade secrets. The provided snippet from California Civil Code 3426.1 defines 'Misappropriation' which involves the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent, acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. The changed text weakens the 'duty to maintain secrecy' by only protecting explicitly marked information, whereas the law protects information regardless of whether it is explicitly marked, if reasonable efforts are made to maintain its secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party is required to disclose any portion of any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party (i) by operation of law or in connection with a judicial or governmental proceeding or arbitration (whether by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process), (ii) pursuant to the rules or regulations of the United States Securities and Exchange Commission (or any other applicable securities regulatory body) or (iii) pursuant to the rules or regulations of any securities exchange on which the Receiving Party’s or its parent company’s securities are listed or similar self- regulatory body, then such disclosure will be permissible only if and after the Receiving Party promptly notifies the Disclosing Party of such requirement (to the extent legally permitted) so that the Disclosing Party (at its sole cost and expense) may seek an appropriate protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party is required to disclose any portion of any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party (i) by operation of law or in connection with a judicial or governmental proceeding or arbitration (whether by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process), (ii) pursuant to the rules or regulations of the United States Securities and Exchange Commission (or any other applicable securities regulatory body) or (iii) pursuant to the rules or regulations of any securities exchange on which the Receiving Party’s or its parent company’s securities are listed or similar self- regulatory body, then such disclosure will be permissible without notifying the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement to notify the disclosing party before revealing confidential information under legal duress, the contract conflicts with established legal norms of protecting trade secrets and complying with discovery obligations while safeguarding sensitive information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change directly contradicts the spirit and application of FRCP 26(c), which allows parties to seek protective orders to limit the scope of discovery and protect confidential information. Notifying the Disclosing Party is crucial for them to seek such protective orders. Removing this step hinders their ability to protect their trade secrets during litigation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Receiving Party's obligation to notify the Disclosing Party before disclosing confidential information under legal duress. The Law Explanation states that this notification is crucial for the Disclosing Party to seek protective orders under FRCP 26(c) to protect their trade secrets during litigation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses Rule 26 and its provisions regarding discovery, reinforcing the importance of protecting information. The original text ensures the Disclosing Party can act to protect its information, while the changed text removes this safeguard, directly contradicting the ability to seek protective measures as intended by Rule 26, thus creating a legal contradiction as the modified version fails to allow for the protection of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: All actions or proceedings arising in connection with, touching upon or relating to this Agreement, the breach thereof and/or the scope of the provisions of this Section 19 other than actions or proceedings seeking equitable relief (a “Proceeding” ) shall be submitted to JAMS ( "JAMS" ) for binding arbitration CONFIDENTIAL 5under its Comprehensive Arbitration Rules and Procedures if the matter in dispute is over $250,000 or under its Streamlined Arbitration Rules and Procedures if the matter in dispute is $250,000 or less (as applicable, the “Rules” ) to be held solely in Los Angeles County, California, U.S.A., in the English language in accordance with the provisions below.
perturbation.changed_text: All actions or proceedings arising in connection with, touching upon or relating to this Agreement, the breach thereof and/or the scope of the provisions of this Section 19 shall be resolved through a coin flip. The decision made by the coin flip will be final and non-appealable.
perturbation.explanation: Replaced binding arbitration with a coin flip. This is not a legally recognized method of dispute resolution. This conflicts with the basic legal requirements for enforceable dispute resolution clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act requires a 'written provision in ... a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract.' A coin flip is not arbitration and does not meet the requirement of a structured dispute resolution process, therefore rendering the agreement unenforceable under the FAA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces binding arbitration with a coin flip, which directly contradicts the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA). Both scraped law snippets, derived from 9 U.S. Code § 2, state that 'A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract...shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable.' Replacing arbitration with a coin flip does not meet the legal standard of settling a controversy by arbitration, thus making the agreement unenforceable under the FAA. The original text specified JAMS arbitration, a structured dispute resolution process, while the changed text entirely abandons this legal requirement in favor of a random outcome, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall commence as of the date first written above and will continue in full force and effect for a period of two (2) years from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall commence as of the date first written above and will continue in full force and effect until the sun explodes.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing a fixed term with an impossible event introduces uncertainty and unenforceability. This conflicts with the requirement for a definite term in contracts, making it difficult to determine the parties' obligations and rights over time.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Contract Law - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While this code relates to definitions related to good faith for the state of California, an indefinite term frustrates the ability to determine the reasonable expectations of the parties involved, violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing which is implied in all contracts under California law. An impossible termination event (the sun exploding) means that, in practical terms, the NDA is perpetual and could impose burdens indefinitely, not allowing for good faith and fair dealing.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a definite contract term (2 years) with an impossible event (sun exploding). This creates an indefinite term, conflicting with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing because it frustrates the ability to determine the reasonable expectations of the parties involved. The Law Explanation states that such an impossible termination event effectively makes the NDA perpetual and imposes burdens indefinitely, thus failing to allow for good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, defines 'agreement', highlighting the importance of understanding the 'bargain of the parties' which is undermined by an indefinite term. The original text provided a clear timeframe, while the changed text introduces uncertainty and unenforceability.
